_description: Actual match outcomes and per-question RBP results from the JTC SportsPredict platform. Joined to our estimates by QUESTION TEXT, not by the platform's own Q-number (its numbering does not match our internal 00_/05_ file order -- e.g. the platform's Q3 is 'England win in regulation', which is Q15 in our own files).

question_results.text: Will Erling Haaland score a goal?
question_results.note: Correct -- crowd was more confidently wrong than us (55% vs our 48%).
England won 1-2 but this question is scoped to regulation only -- Norway evidently led or was level at the 90-minute mark before England's winner (consistent with Q6 tied-at-HT settling YES).

One of the two biggest wins of the match. Validates the mandatory-recheck correction: his 2026 empirical rate was 0.60 (driven by a 2-game hot streak), but his full 2022 WC history (0/5 games with 2+ SOT) and the market (0.28) both said otherwise. Shaded down to 0.32 -- correct, and well clear of the crowd.

question_results.text: Will the match have 3+ total goals?
question_results.note: Final was Norway 1-2 England = 3 goals... see caveat below on regulation-only scoping; this settled NO, meaning regulation goals were 2 or fewer, with any extra goal arriving outside the regulation window this question covers.

question_results.text: Will the match be tied at halftime?

question_results.note: Correct direction, but crowd was closer -- the mandatory-recheck correction (from a raw model of 0.45 up to 0.58) was directionally right but undershot how confident the crowd correctly was.

Validates the SYMMETRIC_REGIME_COVERAGE check's conclusion -- this match's moderate Elo gap (+97.97) meant no downward shading was warranted for Norway's own corner prop, unlike the Switzerland case the same day. Correct.

question_results.text: Will a substitute score or assist?
question_results.note: One of two clear misses this match, and the SAME question type missed the same way in the Argentina-Switzerland match the same day (there: 0.53 vs crowd 0.37, also wrong). The reused 60-match general corpus base rate (0.55) looks miscalibrated specifically for tense knockout-stage matches -- worth building a knockout-stage-only subset next time rather than trusting the all-stages corpus rate as-is. See key_lessons.

question_results.text: Will a penalty be awarded OR a red card shown?
question_results.note: Negligible miss, essentially a coin-flip-level difference from crowd.

question_results.text: Will at least one card be shown in each half?

question_results.note: The other clear miss, and the biggest single loss of the match. This used the near-miss-market-calibration technique (blending a naive Poisson model of 0.7191 with a market-calibrated 0.6460), but landed at 0.68 -- HIGHER than either input, and further from the truth than the crowd's 0.56. The averaging leaned too heavily toward the naive (uncalibrated) model. Lesson: when a near-miss market line disagrees with the naive model, weight the market-calibrated figure more heavily, not evenly -- the same lesson already learned for Switzerland's SOT/corners in the other match, not yet applied here.

question_results.text: Will the second half produce more goals than the first half?
question_results.note: Validates the mandatory-recheck correction of the Monte Carlo sim's uniform-time artifact -- crowd went the other way (53%) and was wrong; our corrected, lower estimate was right.

question_results.text: Will England have more corners AND more shots than Norway?
question_results.note: Near-coin-flip miss on this compound, model-only (no market) question.

key_lessons: Biggest loss: 24+ total shots (-23.83). The near-miss-market-calibration technique (used successfully for Argentina's/Switzerland's corners and shots the same day) was applied here too, but the final blend weighted the naive uncalibrated model too heavily against the market-calibrated figure, pushing the estimate above both inputs and away from the truth.

Second loss: substitute scores/assists (-21.12), and the SAME question missed in the SAME direction (too high) in the Argentina-Switzerland match the same day. This is a real, repeated signal across two independent matches on the same day: the 60-match general ESPN corpus base rate (0.55) for this question type does not hold in tense knockout-stage QFs specifically. Worth building a knockout-stage-only subset of this base rate before the next match, rather than reusing the all-stages figure again.

Biggest win: Bellingham 2+ SOT (+22.27) and second-half-more-goals (+21.71) both directly validate mandatory-recheck corrections made under the restored RULE1 discipline -- in both cases the naive model/sim output was wrong-leaning and the recheck (historical data in Bellingham's case, corpus base rate + market in the second-half case) caught it before submission.
Overall +95.58 RBP, 11/15 beat crowd -- a strong result, and a clean reversal from the Spain-Belgium loss two days prior.
